comments.author: @AndrewSmith-qs1ob
comments.text: I'm an Aussie and I love your narrative. I remember stopping at the remote ghost town of Cook, crossed almost  daily by the Indian Pacific train. I assumed it was named after Captain Cook. But after my visit I thought it more likely to have been named after the verb, to cook 🥵😱. The heat was beyond description - and Cook is only in the southern part of South Australia!!

comments.author: @nexttrip88
comments.text: The Outback is a brutal masterclass in survival, where the environment is "indifferent" to whether you live or die. From extreme heat exceeding 110°F to weaponized creatures like the inland Taipan, it is easily one of the most hostile places on Earth. However, for those who look closely, its ancient geological wonders like Uluru and the Bungle Bungle Range reveal a breathtaking and diverse side of this arid continent

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: The Outback feels like Earth’s way of reminding humans that we are still guests here, not owners. One wrong turn and suddenly a bottle of water becomes more valuable than a luxury car. Funny how deserts can teach life lessons faster than most motivational speakers ever could.

replies.author: @barry7608
replies.text: I have worked and travelled extensively throughout the desert regions and I can assure you a wrong turn can go from a holiday to a tragedy. The distances are huge, the landmarks few and sign post in many many areas non existent. Fuel and water maybe in the 'other' direction. Thanks I love it out there.

comments.author: @barry7608
comments.text: Thanks it's a magical place and I have travelled extensively throughout. Go out to Curtain Springs NT and camp, then go for a night walk and check out the Milky Way stretching from horizon to horizon, its like being among the stars, chilly, sobering and spiritual at least for me.

comments.author: @barry7608
comments.text: A lot of the photography like around Wolf Creek crater was after big rain, the water can be seen in the crater and the shades of green in the background. Never like this 'normally'. In fact the greenery wherever the red soil is seen is following big rains, Ive seen it when its 'normal', being brown and red.

comments.author: @barry7608
comments.text: its a shame you couldn't get the pronunciations right Nullarbor is said Nul a bor. Likewise Galah is said Gal ar.

comments.author: @barry7608
comments.text: Dingoes are NOT dogs, my girl Sandy proved it genetically via cutting edge genetic sequencing in the US, search Sandy Genome. Remember dingoes are NOT dogs, their species is Canus lupus dingo an off shoot of wolves to which they are very similar. Recognised around the world, sadly not by Australian governments.  Save the dingo, highly intelligent and adorable personalities but definitely not as pets. Their needs exceed canine requirements and need devoted care for life, rewarded with a genuine very strong emotional bond. They are extremely trustworthy. 'Pure Dingo' is another search.

comments.author: @Roscoe.P.Coldchain
comments.text: I arrived in Sydney on a one way flight and a years work visa..I only had 800 bucks on arrival I was 24 year old..I arrived with all the intentions to see as much of Australia as I could. But unfortunately it didn’t pan out that way..I was always working and although I did have a few holidays at Byron Bay at Christmas and New Year..But I always had to return to Sydney to earn money..My dream was to visit Cairns and the barrier reef and the outback but i just could never afford it..I,m 55 now living in England and I would love to visit again and see the places I always wanted to go..Beautiful country and beautiful people ♥️♥️..God Bless

You are a wonderful writer, by the way!

comments.author: @WorldDiscovered_Official
comments.text: In my memory, the Outback has always seemed like a very harsh place. I used to think that very little life could survive there, but this documentary shows that it is actually full of life and hidden wonders. From ancient rocks and endless deserts to animals that have adapted in incredible ways, everything was fascinating to watch. This was a really great documentary.
